        have h₆₁ : (volume.restrict (Ioc 0 1)) ≤ volume.restrict (Set.Icc 0 1) := by
          apply MeasureTheory.restrict_le_restrict
          <;> simp [Set.Ioc_subset_Icc_self]
        -- Use the fact that the integral on [0,1] is finite to deduce the integral on (0,1] is finite
        have h₆₂ : HasFiniteIntegral (Φ₁ r) (volume.restrict (Ioc 0 1)) := by
          refine' HasFiniteIntegral.of_le (h₅) _
          <;> simp_all [h₆₁]
          <;>
          (try norm_num) <;>
          (try linarith)
        exact h₆₂
      exact h₆
    exact h₄
  exact h₁

-- We use the fact that Φ₁ is continuous on (0, 1] and the interval [0, 1] is compact to show that Φ₁ is integrable.
  have h₁ : ContinuousOn (Φ₁ r) (Set.Icc 0 1) := by
    -- Prove that Φ₁ is continuous on [0, 1]
    have h₂ : ContinuousOn (Φ₁ r) (Set.Icc 0 1) := by
      -- Use the fact that Φ₁ is continuously differentiable on (0, ∞) and hence continuous on [0, 1]
      apply ContinuousOn.mono (Φ₁_contDiffOn.continuousOn)
      intro x hx
      simp only [Set.mem_Icc, Set.mem_Ioc] at hx ⊢
      -- Show that [0, 1] ⊆ (0, ∞)
      by_cases h : x = 0
      · subst h
        norm_num
        <;>
        (try norm_num) <;>
        (try linarith) <;>
        (try simp_all [hr]) <;>
        (try positivity)
      · -- For x ≠ 0, we have 0 < x ≤ 1
        have h₃ : 0 < x := by
          by_contra h₃
          have h₄ : x ≤ 0 := by linarith
          have h₅ : x = 0 := by linarith
          contradiction
        have h₄ : x ≤ 1 := by linarith
        exact ⟨by linarith, by linarith⟩
    exact h₂
  -- Use the fact that a continuous function on a compact set is integrable
  have h₂ : HasFiniteIntegral (Φ₁ r) (volume.restrict (Ioc 0 1)) := by
    -- Use the fact that Φ₁ is continuous on [0, 1] and hence on (0, 1]
    have h₃ : ContinuousOn (Φ₁ r) (Set.Icc 0 1) := h₁
    -- Use the fact that the restriction of a continuous function to a compact set is integrable
    have h₄ : HasFiniteIntegral (Φ₁ r) (volume.restrict (Icc 0 1)) := by
      -- Use the fact that the interval [0, 1] is compact and Φ₁ is continuous
      apply ContinuousOn.hasFiniteIntegral
      <;> simp_all [measurableSet_Icc]
      <;>
      (try norm_num) <;>
      (try linarith) <;>
      (try simp_all [hr]) <;>
      (try positivity)
    -- Use the fact that the integral over (0, 1] is the same as the integral over [0, 1]
    have h₅ : HasFiniteIntegral (Φ₁ r) (volume.restrict (Ioc 0 1)) := by
      -- Use the fact that the integral over (0, 1] is the same as the integral over [0, 1]
      have h₆ : volume.restrict (Ioc 0 1) = volume.restrict (Icc 0 1) := by
        -- Prove that (0, 1] and [0, 1] have the same measure
        congr 1
        ext x
        simp [Ioc, Icc]
        <;>
        (try norm_num) <;>
        (try linarith) <;>
        (try simp_all [hr]) <;>
        (try positivity)
      rw [h₆]
      exact h₄
    exact h₅
  exact h₂

have h₁ : HasFiniteIntegral (Φ₁ r) (volume.restrict (Ioc 0 1)) := by
    -- Use `continuousOn_of_mem_Ioc` to confirm continuity on the interval (0, 1]
    have h₂ : ContinuousOn (fun x : ℝ => (Φ₁ r) x) (Ioc 0 1) := Φ₁_contDiffOn.continuousOn
    -- Use `hasFiniteIntegral_of_hasIntegral_of_forall_ae` to verify that the integral is finite
    have h₃ : HasFiniteIntegral (fun x : ℝ => (Φ₁ r) x) (volume.restrict (Ioc 0 1)) := by
      -- Since the function is continuous on [0, 1], it is also continuous on (0, 1]
      refine' hasFiniteIntegral_iff_hasFiniteIntegral_restrict_to_mem_of_subset _
        (by exact subset_univ _) _ 
      -- The interval (0, 1] has finite measure
      <;> try norm_num <;>
      (try
        apply ContinuousOn.hasFiniteIntegral_on_Ioc) <;>
      (try
        {
          apply ContinuousOn.hasFiniteIntegral_on_Icc
          <;> try continuity
        }) <;>
      (try
        {
          apply Continuous.integrableOn_Icc
          <;> try continuity
        }) <;>
      (try
        {
          norm_num
          <;>
          continuity
        }) <;>
      (try
        {
          exact Φ₁_contDiffOn.continuousOn
        }) <;>
      (try
        {
          exact Φ₁_contDiffOn.continuousOn
        })
      <;>
      (try
        {
          norm_num
          <;>
          continuity
        })
      <;>
      (try
        {
          norm_num
          <;>
          exact Φ₁_contDiffOn.continuousOn
        })
    exact h₃
  exact h₁

have h₁ : HasFiniteIntegral (Φ₁ r) (volume.restrict (Ioc 0 1)) := by
    -- Use the fact that Φ₁ is continuous and bounded on [0,1] to show that it has a finite integral
    have h₂ : ContinuousOn (Φ₁ r) (Icc 0 1) := by
      apply ContinuousOn.mono (Φ₁_contDiffOn.continuousOn)
      intro x hx
      simp only [Ioc_mem_nhds, Icc_subset_Ioc, le_of_lt] at hx ⊢
      <;> simp_all [Set.mem_Icc]
      <;> linarith
    -- Use the fact that Φ₁ is continuous on [0,1] to show that it is bounded
    have h₃ : BddAbove (Set.range (fun x : ℝ => Φ₁ r x)) := by
      -- Since Φ₁ is continuous on [0,1], it attains its maximum
      have h₄ : IsCompact (Set.Icc 0 1) := isCompact_Icc
      have h₅ : ContinuousOn (Φ₁ r) (Set.Icc 0 1) := h₂
      have h₆ : BddAbove (Set.range (fun x : ℝ => Φ₁ r x)) := by
        -- Use the extreme value theorem to show that Φ₁ is bounded above
        have h₇ : BddAbove (Set.image (Φ₁ r) (Set.Icc 0 1)) := by
          apply isCompact_bddAbove_image h₄ h₅
        have h₈ : Set.image (Φ₁ r) (Set.Icc 0 1) ⊆ Set.range (fun x : ℝ => Φ₁ r x) := by
          intro y hy
          rcases hy with ⟨x, hx, rfl⟩
          exact ⟨x, rfl⟩
        exact Set.BddAbove.mono h₈ h₇
      exact h₆
    -- Use the fact that Φ₁ is bounded and continuous to show that it has a finite integral
    have h₄ : HasFiniteIntegral (Φ₁ r) (volume.restrict (Ioc 0 1)) := by
      -- Use the fact that Φ₁ is continuous on [0,1] and bounded to show that it has a finite integral
      have h₅ : ContinuousOn (Φ₁ r) (Ioc 0 1) := by
        apply ContinuousOn.mono (Φ₁_contDiffOn.continuousOn)
        intro x hx
        simp only [Ioc_mem_nhds, Icc_subset_Ioc, le_of_lt] at hx ⊢
        <;> simp_all [Set.mem_Ioc]
        <;> linarith
      -- Use the fact that Φ₁ is continuous on (0,1] and bounded to show that it has a finite integral
      have h₆ : HasFiniteIntegral (Φ₁ r) (volume.restrict (Ioc 0 1)) := by
        -- Use the fact that Φ₁ is continuous on (0,1] and bounded to show that it has a finite integral
        apply ContinuousOn.hasFiniteIntegral
        <;> (try norm_num) <;> (try simp_all [measurableSet_Ioc])
        <;> (try
          {
            -- Use the fact that Φ₁ is bounded to show that it has a finite integral
            have h₇ : BddAbove (Set.range (fun x : ℝ => Φ₁ r x)) := h₃
            have h₈ : BddBelow (Set.range (fun x : ℝ => Φ₁ r x)) := by
              -- Since Φ₁ is continuous on [0,1], it attains its minimum
              have h₉ : IsCompact (Set.Icc 0 1) := isCompact_Icc
              have h₁₀ : ContinuousOn (Φ₁ r) (Set.Icc 0 1) := h₂
              have h₁₁ : BddBelow (Set.image (Φ₁ r) (Set.Icc 0 1)) := by
                apply isCompact_bddBelow_image h₉ h₁₀
              have h₁₂ : Set.image (Φ₁ r) (Set.Icc 0 1) ⊆ Set.range (fun x : ℝ => Φ₁ r x) := by
                intro y hy
                rcases hy with ⟨x, hx, rfl⟩
                exact ⟨x, rfl⟩
              exact Set.BddBelow.mono h₁₂ h₁₁
            -- Use the fact that Φ₁ is bounded to show that it has a finite integral
            exact ⟨by
              -- Use the fact that Φ₁ is bounded above to show that it has a finite integral
              obtain ⟨M, hM⟩ := h₃
              refine' ⟨-M, _⟩
              rintro _ ⟨x, rfl⟩
              have h₁₃ : Φ₁ r x ≤ M := by
                have h₁₄ : Φ₁ r x ∈ Set.range (fun x : ℝ => Φ₁ r x) := by
                  exact ⟨x, rfl⟩
                have h₁₅ : Φ₁ r x ≤ M := by
                  exact hM h₁₄
                exact h₁₅
              have h₁₆ : -M ≤ Φ₁ r x := by
                obtain ⟨m, hm⟩ := h₈
                have h₁₇ : m ≤ Φ₁ r x := by
                  have h₁₈ : Φ₁ r x ∈ Set.range (fun x : ℝ => Φ₁ r x) := by
                    exact ⟨x, rfl⟩
                  have h₁₉ : m ≤ Φ₁ r x := by
                    exact hm h₁₈
                  exact h₁₉
                linarith
              linarith, by
              -- Use the fact that Φ₁ is bounded below to show that it has a finite integral
              obtain ⟨M, hM⟩ := h₃
              refine' ⟨M, _⟩
              rintro _ ⟨x, rfl⟩
              have h₁₃ : Φ₁ r x ≤ M := by
                have h₁₄ : Φ₁ r x ∈ Set.range (fun x : ℝ => Φ₁ r x) := by
                  exact ⟨x, rfl⟩
                have h₁₅ : Φ₁ r x ≤ M := by
                  exact hM h₁₄
                exact h₁₅
              linarith⟩
          })
        <;> (try simp_all [measurableSet_Ioc])
        <;> (try norm_num)
        <;> (try linarith)
      exact h₆
    exact h₄
  exact h₁
